title: Greedy regex in _phase_trend_scan silently drops trends when LLM output contains brackets
description:
In runner.py _phase_trend_scan() (line ~293), the fallback JSON extraction uses:

    match = re.search(r'[\[\s\S]*\]', output)

The greedy [\s\S]* matches from the first '[' to the LAST ']' in the entire output. If the LLM wraps the JSON array in commentary containing bracket characters (e.g., 'Here are the trends [as requested]:\n[...actual JSON...]'), the regex captures everything from the first '[' to the final ']', including non-JSON text. json.loads() then fails, and self.trends silently falls back to [].

This is the same class of bug as 2026-04-01-007, which fixed greedy regex in _parse_findings by replacing it with balanced-brace extraction. The _phase_trend_scan code was not updated.

Impact: when the trend_scan LLM produces valid JSON wrapped in commentary, trends are silently set to [] — research agents run without trend context, reducing finding relevance. This is intermittent and hard to diagnose because the phase doesn't fail (it logs 'Trend scan: 0 topics identified' which looks normal).
requirements: Replace the greedy regex r'[\[\s\S]*\]' in _phase_trend_scan with balanced-bracket extraction that handles nested arrays correctly
Reuse or adapt the _extract_balanced_json_blocks pattern from orchestrator/agents.py for bracket extraction
Add a helper function _extract_balanced_json_arrays(text) to agents.py alongside existing _extract_balanced_json_blocks
Log a WARNING when the fallback extraction is needed (direct json.loads failed) so trend parsing issues are visible
done_criteria: Trend scan output with brackets in surrounding commentary still parses the JSON array correctly
Trend scan output with nested arrays parses correctly
All existing tests pass (python3 -m pytest tests/ -x -q)
context: Same bug class as 2026-04-01-007 (greedy regex in _parse_findings, now fixed with balanced-brace extraction). The trend_scan path uses the same greedy pattern but for arrays instead of objects. The fix should be consistent with the _parse_findings approach.
